desc: The native "ladybug." Orange with black spots and converging white thoracic lines. Major aphid predator. Aggregates by millions in mountain canyons in winter.

fm.Color: Orange with 13 black spots; white converging thoracic lines
fm.Behavior: Aggregates in millions in mountain canyons for winter dormancy; important aphid predator
fm.Season: Year-round; disperses from mountain aggregations in spring

desc: Introduced European species. Larger than native ladybugs. Seven spots on red elytra.

fm.Behavior: Voracious aphid predator

desc: Invasive. Extremely variable color/pattern. Aggregates in buildings in fall. Outcompeting natives.

fm.Color: Highly variable — orange to red with 0–22 spots
fm.Behavior: Aggregates in buildings; bites when handled; bad taste/smell; displacing natives
fm.Diet: Aphids, but also native ladybug larvae

desc: Large, spectacular beetle attracted to lights on summer nights. White-striped. Hisses/squeaks when handled.

fm.Color: Brown with 10 bold white longitudinal stripes
fm.Behavior: Attracted to lights; hisses/squeaks when handled; strong clumsy flier; males have huge lamellate antennae
fm.Habitat: Sandy soil (larvae are root grubs); urban lights at night
fm.Diet: Larvae: plant roots. Adults: do not feed.
fm.Season: Jun–Aug; adults to lights summer nights

desc: Largest beetle in western NA. Males with spectacular horn. Rare in LA Co. — mountain oak woodlands.

fm.Behavior: ♂ uses horn for fighting over ♀; nocturnal; attracted to lights; larva in decaying wood 2+ years
fm.Diet: Larvae: rotting wood. Adults: tree sap, fruit.

desc: The large, iridescent green beetle that bumbles loudly through LA backyards in summer. A SoCal classic.

fm.Color: Bright metallic green dorsally; iridescent green ventrally
fm.Behavior: Loud clumsy flight; bumbles into things; attracted to ripe fruit; larvae are "crawly backs" in compost
fm.Habitat: Urban gardens, orchards — anywhere with ripe fruit or compost
fm.Diet: Adults: ripe fruit, sap. Larvae: decomposing organic matter.

desc: Very similar to Figeater but slightly more northern distribution. Hard to distinguish without examination.

fm.Behavior: Like Figeater

desc: Massive longhorn beetle. Males fly to lights on summer nights. Larvae bore in tree roots for 3–5 years.

fm.Color: Dark brown to black; robust; saw-toothed antennae
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; males fly to lights; females sluggish; larvae in tree roots 3–5 years
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, orchards, urban near trees
fm.Diet: Larvae: live tree roots (can damage orchards). Adults: do not feed.

desc: Stunning — black and white banded longhorn. One of the most beautiful NA beetles. Associated with hardwoods.

fm.Color: White/gray with black bands; long antennae banded black and white
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; distinctive color makes it unmistakable
fm.Habitat: Riparian with alder, willow, ash; mountain canyons

desc: Brilliant metallic green/gold. Larvae bore in dead conifers. One of the flashiest beetles in NA.

fm.Color: Brilliant metallic green with gold/copper margins
fm.Behavior: Fast flier in sunny weather; attracted to freshly cut or fire-damaged wood; larvae in dead conifers
fm.Habitat: Coniferous forest, lumber yards, buildings with untreated wood
fm.Diet: Larvae: dead conifer wood. Adults: flower pollen.

desc: Flattened metallic beetle. Major pest of fruit and shade trees. Larvae bore under bark.

fm.Behavior: Sunbathes on tree bark; larvae bore spiral galleries under bark

desc: Distinctive "click" mechanism — flips itself into air when on back. Large eyed-click beetle with false eyespots.

fm.Color: Black with two large white-rimmed false eyespots on pronotum
fm.Behavior: Click mechanism: thoracic spine snaps into groove, launching beetle into air with audible click
fm.Diet: Larvae (wireworms): plant roots. Adults: plant sap.

desc: Bright red-orange and black. Contains cantharidin toxin. Desert spring swarms on brittlebush.

fm.Color: Bright red-orange head/thorax; metallic black elytra
fm.Behavior: Swarms on Encelia flowers in desert spring; bleeds cantharidin from joints if harassed (blistering agent)
fm.Habitat: Desert, coastal sage — on Encelia, Larrea
fm.Diet: Adults: flowers/pollen. Larvae: parasitic on ground bee nests.
fm.Season: Mar–May; mass emergence after desert rains

desc: ♀ is bioluminescent larva-like (neotenic). ♂ is typical beetle that flies to lights. ♀ glows green from lateral organs.

fm.Size: ♂: ½ in–⅔ in. ♀: 1–2.5 in (larval form; neotenic).
fm.Color: ♂: brown, typical beetle. ♀: wingless, larval form, glows green.
fm.Behavior: ♀ bioluminescent — glows green from lateral light organs; attracts ♂; predator of millipedes
fm.Season: Oct–Nov; ♂ to lights fall; ♀ glow on ground

desc: Desert darkling covered in waxy blue-white coating. Famous for being virtually uncrushed — can withstand forces 39,000x body weight.

fm.Color: Blue-white waxy coating over black body
fm.Behavior: Extremely hard exoskeleton — studied by material scientists; wax coating reduces water loss in desert
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub — Mojave and Colorado deserts; NE LA County
fm.Diet: Detritivore — fungi, lichens, dead plant material

desc: Bright red-orange with black spots. Feeds on milkweed — sequesters cardiac glycosides for defense. Warning coloration.

fm.Color: Bright red-orange with 4 black spots (each eye is split by antenna base — hence "tetrophthalmus")
fm.Behavior: Feeds on milkweed; warning coloration (toxic); squeaks when handled
fm.Habitat: Anywhere Asclepias grows — gardens, roadsides, meadows

desc: Unusual ladybug — pale with sparse spots. Associated with pines. Feeds on aphids on conifers.

fm.Color: Pale cream/tan with sparse dark spots
fm.Behavior: Found on pine/fir needles; feeds on conifer aphids

desc: Large beetle. ♂ with huge mandibles used in combat. Larvae in rotting oak wood.

fm.Size: 1–1.5 in (♂ with mandibles up to 2 in)
fm.Color: Dark brown to black; large mandibles (♂)
fm.Behavior: ♂ combat over ♀ using mandibles; nocturnal; to lights; larvae in rotting wood 2+ years
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, rotting stumps and logs
fm.Diet: Larvae: decaying oak wood. Adults: tree sap.

desc: Spectacular — metallic silver-green with green stripes. One of most beautiful NA beetles. Associated with juniper at elevation.

fm.Color: Brilliant metallic silver-green with green stripes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; feeds on juniper foliage; larvae in soil
fm.Habitat: Juniper/oak woodland above 5,000' (edge of range in LA mountains)

desc: CA's diurnal "firefly" — does NOT glow. Dark with orange thorax. Feeds on tree sap and pollen. True bioluminescent fireflies are absent from most of SoCal.

fm.Color: Black elytra; orange/red thorax with dark center
fm.Behavior: Diurnal (unlike eastern fireflies); does not produce light; feeds on sap/pollen

desc: Spectacular dung beetle — metallic green/blue/copper. ♂ with horn. At extreme western range edge in SoCal.

fm.Color: Metallic green, blue, copper; ♂ with curved horn
fm.Behavior: Buries dung for larvae; tunneler (not roller)
fm.Habitat: Open areas with large mammal dung (range edge)

desc: Predator of bark beetles. Bright red/blue-black checkered. Beneficial pest control agent in forests.

fm.Color: Bright red pronotum; blue-black checkered elytra
fm.Behavior: Hunts bark beetles on tree bark; both adults and larvae are predators
fm.Habitat: Pine and conifer forests; bark of infested trees
fm.Season: Year-round; peaks during bark beetle outbreaks

desc: Tiny beetle that changes color — from metallic gold to red-orange when disturbed. Miniature jewel on morning glory leaves.

fm.Color: Metallic gold (at rest) → red-orange (disturbed); transparent margins
fm.Behavior: Color-changing — gold to red when stressed; on morning glory leaves; larvae carry fecal shield
fm.Habitat: Gardens — on Convolvulaceae (morning glory, sweet potato)

desc: Yellow-green with 12 black spots. Major agricultural pest. Larva is the "southern corn rootworm."

fm.Behavior: Feeds on flowers and leaves of many plants; transmits bacterial wilt
fm.Habitat: Agricultural fields, gardens — anywhere with crops

desc: Tiny but ecologically critical bark beetle. Mass kills pines during drought. Creates "pitch tubes" on bark.

fm.Behavior: Mass pheromone-coordinated attacks on drought-stressed pines; creates pitch tubes

desc: The "woodworm" — larvae bore tiny round holes in dry wood. Common household pest in older structures.

fm.Behavior: Larvae bore in dry wood for years; create <⅛ in round exit holes; "death watch" tapping

desc: Invasive eucalyptus pest.

fm.Behavior: Eucalyptus defoliator; invasive

desc: Entirely spotless orange-red.

fm.Behavior: Aphid predator; beneficial

desc: Nearly uncrushed exoskeleton.

fm.Behavior: Plays dead; studied for crush-resistant engineering

desc: Dense golden-brown hair.

fm.Behavior: Headstand defense; flightless

desc: Common foothill trail beetle.

fm.Behavior: Headstand; quinone spray

desc: Major household pest. Destroys woolens.

fm.Behavior: Adults on flowers; larvae destroy fibers

desc: Soft-bodied flower beetle.

fm.Behavior: Predator + pollinator on flowers

desc: Eats mildew, not aphids.

fm.Behavior: Mildew feeder; beneficial

desc: On Solanaceae. Fecal shield larvae.

fm.Behavior: Larvae carry fecal shield

desc: Invasive eucalyptus pest.

fm.Behavior: Eucalyptus defoliator

desc: Introduced. Under rocks.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal predator

desc: Red/blue-black. Larvae eat bee larvae.

fm.Behavior: Adults pollinate; larvae parasitize bee nests

desc: Invasive.

fm.Behavior: Eucalyptus pest

desc: Biocontrol agent. Larvae mimic prey.

fm.Behavior: Larvae look like mealybugs they eat

desc: -spotted flower longhorn.

fm.Behavior: On chaparral flowers

desc: White grubs in turf.

fm.Behavior: Adults to lights; larvae damage turf

desc: All-female. Flightless.

fm.Behavior: Parthenogenetic; notches leaf margins

desc: Metallic green + red head. Cantharidin.

fm.Behavior: Mass emergence on wildflowers

desc: Two color forms. Psyllid predator.

fm.Color: Gray with spots OR black with 2 red spots
fm.Behavior: Predator; beneficial

desc: Brilliant metallic cobalt. On milkweed.

fm.Behavior: Sequesters cardiac glycosides

desc: Native. Spotless. Declining.

fm.Behavior: Declining — invasive competition

desc: Saved CA citrus industry 1889.

fm.Behavior: Famous biocontrol of cottony cushion scale

desc: Related to Diabolical. Under oak bark.

fm.Behavior: Under dead oak bark; plays dead

desc: Bioluminescent ♀ glows pink. SoCal's "firefly."

fm.Behavior: ♀ glows on ground fall evenings; ♂ flies to find glowing ♀

desc: Raises tail scorpion-like. Introduced.

fm.Behavior: Scorpion-pose threat; bites; nocturnal

desc: Metallic green predator. Stream margins.

fm.Behavior: Fast runner; active predator

desc: Yellow-green with 3 black stripes. Feeds on cucurbits. Transmits bacterial wilt.

fm.Behavior: Feeds on cucurbit flowers and leaves; disease vector

desc: Major stored-product pest. Tiny red-brown beetle in flour, cereal, grain. Model organism in ecology research.

fm.Behavior: Infests stored grain/flour; model organism in ecology

desc: Tiny black flea beetle devastating to Brassicaceae seedlings. Shot-hole damage diagnostic.

fm.Behavior: Massive damage to Brassicaceae seedlings; jumps when disturbed

desc: Pale tan chafer on roses and other ornamentals. Long legs. Swarms flowers in spring.

fm.Behavior: Swarms roses and ornamental flowers; clumsy flier

desc: Federally Threatened. Green with red wing margins. Obligate on elderberry. Central Valley species at range edge in LA.

fm.Color: Metallic green with red/orange wing margins
fm.Behavior: Adults on elderberry flowers; larvae bore in elderberry stems 1–2 years
fm.Diet: Larvae: elderberry pith. Adults: elderberry flowers

desc: Large, metallic ground beetle. Caterpillar hunter — climbs trees to eat tent caterpillars. Important biocontrol.

fm.Color: Black with green/bronze metallic sheen; robust
fm.Behavior: Climbs trees to hunt caterpillars; nocturnal; aggressive predator
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, orchards — wherever caterpillar outbreaks occur
fm.Diet: Predator — caterpillars (major biocontrol of tent caterpillars)

desc: Large, hairy rove beetle attracted to carrion. Short wing covers expose flexible abdomen. Important forensic indicator.

fm.Color: Black with gray hairy bands; short wing covers
fm.Behavior: At carrion; raises tail scorpion-like; predator of maggots; forensic indicator

desc: Fuzzy, bumblebee-mimicking flower beetle. Buzzy flight. Visits flowers and ripe fruit. Smaller than Figeater.

fm.Behavior: Buzzy flight among flowers; visits ripe fruit; bumblebee mimic
fm.Habitat: Gardens, orchards, meadows — flowers and fruit

desc: Black weevil that kills agave and yucca. Major pest of century plants. Larvae bore into heart.

fm.Behavior: Bores into agave/yucca heart; can kill mature plants

desc: Massive longhorn beetle — up to 3 in. Larvae in palo verde roots for years. Adults fly to lights on summer nights. Desert species reaching LA.

fm.Color: Dark brown; robust; long antennae; spined thorax
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flies to lights summer; larvae in palo verde roots 3+ years; harmless
fm.Habitat: Desert — wherever palo verde trees grow
fm.Diet: Larvae: palo verde roots. Adults: do not feed
fm.Season: Jun–Aug; adults at lights summer nights

desc: Common desert darkling beetle with prominent lateral spine on thorax. Headstand defense sprays quinones.

fm.Behavior: Headstand defense; quinone spray; flightless; trails at dusk

desc: Golden-yellow scarab. Nocturnal; attracted to lights. Larvae feed on roots.

fm.Shape: Golden-yellow scarab

desc: Black beetle with red-orange spots. Found on shelf fungi and bracket mushrooms.

fm.Shape: Black beetle with red-orange spots

desc: Metallic green blister beetle. Produces cantharidin. Swarms on desert flowers after rain.

fm.Habitat: Swarms on desert flowers after rain

desc: Metallic violet-blue blister beetle. Hypermetamorphic — larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees.

fm.Shape: Metallic violet-blue blister beetle

desc: Tiny bark beetle found under bark of dead oaks and conifers. Predator of wood-boring larvae.

fm.Shape: Tiny bark beetle found under bark of dead oaks and conifers

desc: Large brown longhorn of conifer forests. Larvae bore in dead pine and fir.

fm.Habitat: Large brown longhorn of conifer forests

desc: Introduced snout beetle. Flightless pest of ornamental plants. Nocturnal.
Inflated blue-black blister beetle of Mojave margins. Walks slowly with abdomen raised.

fm.Shape: Inflated blue-black blister beetle of Mojave margins

desc: Bright red flattened beetle under bark of dead trees. Larvae freeze-tolerant.

fm.Shape: Bright red flattened beetle under bark of dead trees

desc: Elongate weevil with snout longer than body. Breeds in hardwood logs. Males fight over females.

fm.Shape: Elongate weevil with snout longer than body

desc: Ancient relict beetle of cold mountain streams. Poor swimmer — clings to rocks in rapids.

fm.Habitat: Ancient relict beetle of cold mountain streams

desc: Black and yellow leaf beetle on willows and poplars. Larvae and adults skeletonize leaves.

fm.Shape: Black and yellow leaf beetle on willows and poplars

desc: Flightless black longhorn on prickly pear and cholla. Larvae bore into cactus pads.

fm.Shape: Flightless black longhorn on prickly pear and cholla

desc: Green with cream stripes. Found on cucurbit flowers in summer gardens.

fm.Habitat: Found on cucurbit flowers in summer gardens

desc: Invasive metallic green jewel beetle devastating ash trees. Not yet confirmed in LA but actively monitored.
Orange with 12 black spots. Pest of asparagus. European introduction.
Shiny black with two red spots. Scale insect specialist. Important biological control agent.

fm.Shape: Shiny black with two red spots

desc: Tiny iridescent blue-green beetle that jumps when disturbed. On evening primrose and willowherb.

fm.Shape: Tiny iridescent blue-green beetle that jumps when disturbed

desc: Large aquatic beetle to 1.5 in. Streamlined for swimming. Both larvae and adults are voracious predators.
Social beetle living in rotting logs. Parents and larvae communicate by stridulation. At western range limit.

fm.Shape: Social beetle living in rotting logs

desc: Green beetle — larva is billion-dollar crop pest. Adults feed on corn silk and pollen.

fm.Shape: Green beetle — larva is billion-dollar crop pest

desc: Small iridescent scarab — males silver-green. Swarms on roses and light-colored flowers in spring.

fm.Shape: Small iridescent scarab — males silver-green

desc: Brown scarab with white stripes. Hisses when handled by forcing air through spiracles. Mountain areas.
Small dark tiger beetle with white spots. Fast predator of open ground. Larva ambushes from burrow.

fm.Shape: Small dark tiger beetle with white spots

desc: Green tiger beetle of sandy creek banks. Extremely fast runner — one of fastest insects relative to body size.

fm.Behavior: Extremely fast runner; ambush from burrow as larva

desc: Dark bronze tiger beetle of mountain trails. Active on hot sunny days.

fm.Habitat: Dark bronze tiger beetle of mountain trails

desc: Small red-orange and black beetle. Serious pest of asparagus. European introduction. Larvae on stems.
Large predatory rove beetle with short elytra. Gold-spotted. Hunts caterpillars and grubs.

fm.Shape: Large predatory rove beetle with short elytra

desc: Orange with expanded net-veined elytra. Toxic — model for mimicry ring. Found on flowers.

fm.Shape: Orange with expanded net-veined elytra

desc: Soft-bodied yellow-orange beetle abundant on flowers. Predator and pollinator. Common in fall.

fm.Shape: Soft-bodied yellow-orange beetle abundant on flowers

desc: Large golden-tan scarab with black spots. Larvae in rotting wood. Adults on grape leaves. At range edge.

fm.Shape: Large golden-tan scarab with black spots

desc: Yellow-green beetle defoliating elm trees. European introduction. Both adults and larvae feed on leaves.
Flightless scarab emerging only during fall rains. Larvae feed on roots for 10+ years underground.

fm.Shape: Flightless scarab emerging only during fall rains

desc: Tiny beetle infesting stored dried fruit, nuts, and fermenting produce. Cosmopolitan pest.
Small dark beetle. Larvae eat wool, silk, animal products. More damaging than varied carpet beetle.
Tiny brown beetle infesting dried foods, spices, pharmaceuticals, books. Eats almost anything organic.
Flightless black longhorn on prickly pear and cholla. Larvae bore into cactus pads.

fm.Shape: Flightless black longhorn on prickly pear and cholla

desc: Small black dung beetle rolling balls of dung. Important nutrient recycler. Found in grasslands.

fm.Shape: Small black dung beetle rolling balls of dung

desc: Bark beetle of pines. Galleries create characteristic fan pattern under bark. Pheromone-mediated mass attacks.

fm.Shape: Bark beetle of pines

desc: Shiny black beetle spinning on water surface. Split eyes see above and below water simultaneously.

fm.Shape: Shiny black beetle spinning on water surface

desc: Large aquatic beetle. Streamlined predator — hunts tadpoles, small fish. Breathes air trapped under wings.
Tiny metallic blue-green beetle jumping when disturbed. Skeletonizes willow and alder leaves.

fm.Shape: Tiny metallic blue-green beetle jumping when disturbed

desc: Small weevil on prickly pear cactus. Larvae bore into cactus pads. Important cactus herbivore.

fm.Shape: Small weevil on prickly pear cactus

desc: Small jewel beetle with yellow markings. Larvae bore in dead branches. Adults on flowers.

fm.Shape: Small jewel beetle with yellow markings

desc: Sprays boiling hot quinone from abdomen at 100°C with audible pop. Remarkable chemical defense.

fm.Shape: Sprays boiling hot quinone from abdomen at 100°C with audible pop

desc: Orange ladybug with parenthesis-shaped marks on pronotum. Native. Important aphid predator.

fm.Shape: Orange ladybug with parenthesis-shaped marks on pronotum

desc: Tiny brown beetle infesting stored tobacco, spices, dried foods. One of most destructive pantry pests.
Extremely small beetle in stored grain. One of the smallest beetles encountered in homes.
Carrion-feeding beetle used by museums to clean bones. Also infests stored products. Important forensic indicator.
Red and black predatory beetle hunting bark beetles on conifers. Important natural biocontrol agent.

fm.Shape: Red and black predatory beetle hunting bark beetles on conifers

desc: Large black ground beetle with massive mandibles. Burrows in garden soil. Nocturnal predator of larvae and slugs.
Unusual plant-eating ladybug (most are predators). Orange with 8 spots per elytron. Pest of beans.

fm.Color: Orange with 16 black spots — resembles ladybug
fm.Behavior: Herbivorous — unusual for a ladybug

desc: Iridescent green-copper scarab. Not yet established in LA but under quarantine surveillance. Devastating turf pest.

fm.Origin: Japanese — under active quarantine in CA

desc: Large dark jewel beetle with sculptured texture. Larvae bore in dead pines. Adults on felled logs.

fm.Habitat: Pine forests — dead and dying trees

desc: Black flightless beetle that raises abdomen (headstanding) when threatened. Desert darkling — common on trails.

fm.Color: Black; smooth or rough depending on species
fm.Defense: Headstand display — sprays quinones from abdomen

desc: Pointed-tail darkling beetle. Common desert species. Headstands when disturbed.
Smooth-shelled relative of the warty ironclad. Blue-white waxy coating. Desert species.
Bioluminescent beetle. Female larviform with glowing segments. Male winged with enormous antennae.

fm.Behavior: Bioluminescent females attract males

desc: Brown longhorn with ivory spots. Larvae bore in hardwoods. Can emerge from furniture years after milling.

fm.Behavior: Larvae survive in processed wood for years

desc: Brilliant green and gold ground beetle. Nocturnal caterpillar hunter. One of most beautiful NA beetles.

fm.Behavior: Climbs trees for caterpillars; attracted to lights

desc: Large click beetle with prominent eyespots on pronotum. Western relative of A. oculatus. Click escape mechanism.

fm.Color: Black with white mottling; large eyespots

desc: Yellow and black longhorn beetle common on flowers. Larvae bore in dead hardwoods.
Small rounded darkling beetle of coastal sand dunes. Declining with habitat loss. Nocturnal.
Large flightless longhorn on chollas. Stouter than M. appressum. Desert specialist.
Dark bronze-brown jewel beetle. Major pest of fruit and shade trees — larvae bore under bark.
Brilliant green tiger beetle. European species occasionally intercepted. Fast predator of open ground.

fm.Color: Bright metallic green with cream spots

desc: Devastating invasive bark beetle spreading through LA's urban forest since 2012. Farms Fusarium fungus inside tree trunks. Over 60 tree species affected.

fm.Damage: Farms Fusarium fungus inside trees; kills host

desc: Invasive jewel beetle killing oaks. Larvae bore D-shaped galleries under bark. Spreading toward LA from San Diego.

fm.Color: Dark green-black with gold spots on elytra
fm.Damage: D-shaped exit holes; canopy dieback; tree death

desc: Striking large beach-dwelling rove beetle. Sand-colored with dark mottling — superb camouflage on wet sand. Nocturnal predator on beach hoppers. California endemic.

fm.Color: Sand with dark mottling; flattened body

desc: Yellow-orange with black stripes. Body produces cantharidin — causes blisters on skin contact. Larvae parasitize grasshopper eggs (beneficial). Adults eat flowers.

fm.Warning: Produces cantharidin — causes skin blisters on contact
fm.Habitat: Fields, gardens — adults on flowers; larvae parasitize grasshopper eggs

desc: Large, brilliantly colored blister beetle — black with bold yellow-orange cross pattern. Desert edge. Larvae parasitize solitary bee nests. Cantharidin producer.

fm.Color: Black with bold yellow-orange iron-cross pattern
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy areas — spring emergence

desc: Broad flat beetle with prominent elytral ridges. Found at carrion and decaying mushrooms. An important decomposer. Mites hitchhike on adults (phoresy).

fm.Color: Dark with reddish-brown pronotum; ridged elytra

desc: Small gray-brown weevil that chews notches in leaf margins of roses, citrus, and many ornamentals. Nocturnal. Flightless — all females, reproduces by parthenogenesis.

fm.Color: Gray-brown with pale diagonal stripe on elytra
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flightless; all-female (parthenogenetic)

desc: Metallic blue-green rove beetle common at carrion and compost. One of the most frequently encountered rove beetles in urban LA.
Tiny metallic blue-green jumping beetle. 'Flea' refers to powerful jumping ability. Larvae and adults create shot-hole damage on grape and alder leaves.

fm.Habitat: Grape, alder, willows — shot-hole leaf damage
fm.Behavior: Powerful jumping ability like a flea

desc: Brown longhorn with tan zigzag bands. Common on recently killed oaks. Emerges from firewood — often the beetle crawling out of your fireplace woodpile.

fm.Habitat: Dead oak wood — emerges from firewood

desc: Metallic green-bronze jewel beetle. Larvae bore in incense cedar, juniper. Adults bask on sun-heated bark. Iridescent coloring makes them gems of the insect world.

fm.Habitat: Cedar, juniper forest — adults on sun-heated bark

desc: Large shiny black ground beetle common under debris in gardens. Nocturnal predator and seed-eater. Attracted to lights. One of the most common large beetles in urban LA.
Large longhorn beetle devastating drought-stressed eucalyptus since 1984. Larvae bore under bark. Biocontrol by parasitoid wasp Avetianella longoi partially successful.

fm.Color: Dark brown with pale zigzag markings; very long antennae

desc: One of the largest aquatic beetles in North America (up to 1.5 in). Swims using alternate leg strokes (vs. synchronized in diving beetles). Adults attracted to lights.

fm.Color: Dark olive-black; streamlined; keel on underside
fm.Habitat: Ponds, streams — attracted to lights at night
fm.Behavior: Alternate leg swimming (vs. synchronized in Dytiscidae)

desc: Small shiny black beetle with truncated elytra (abdomen exposed). Predator of fly larvae in carrion and dung. Important forensic entomology indicator.

fm.Color: Shiny jet black; short elytra; compact oval
fm.Habitat: Carrion, dung, compost — predatory on fly larvae

desc: Tiny brown beetle whose larvae reduce hardwood to powder. Major pest of hardwood flooring, furniture, and lumber. Fine powder ('frass') falling from tiny exit holes diagnostic.

fm.Damage: Larvae reduce wood to powder; tiny exit holes + frass

desc: Large orange-and-black carrion beetle. Buries small animal carcasses underground to provision larvae. Both parents provide extended biparental care — unusual for beetles.

fm.Color: Black with orange-red markings; large clubbed antennae
fm.Habitat: Open areas — buries small animal carcasses
fm.Behavior: Biparental care; parents feed larvae on carrion

desc: Small dung beetle — ♂ has two curved horns used in combat for mates. Introduced from Mediterranean. Tunnels beneath dung pats; important nutrient recycler.

fm.Color: Dark bronze-brown; ♂ with two curved horns
fm.Habitat: Pastures, open areas — tunnels beneath dung
fm.Behavior: ♂ horns for mate combat; important nutrient recycler

desc: Heavy reddish-brown beetle attracted to lights en masse on warm spring nights. 'May beetles' or 'June bugs.' Larvae (white grubs) damage turf. 3-year life cycle.

fm.Habitat: Urban — attracted to lights; larvae in turf

desc: Tiny ant-like beetle found on flowers. Actually predatory on small insects and mites. Narrow waist resembles ant. Very common but easily overlooked.

fm.Habitat: Flowers, leaf litter — predatory on small arthropods

desc: Small humpbacked beetle that tumbles rapidly when disturbed — somersaults off flowers. Pointed abdomen projects beyond wing tips. Larvae bore in plant stems.

fm.Behavior: Rapid tumbling/spinning escape behavior

desc: Tiny beetle that develops entirely inside dried beans and peas. Major stored-product pest. Adults emerge from round exit holes in infested legumes. Cosmopolitan.

fm.Color: Mottled brown with pale elytra patches
fm.Habitat: Stored dried beans, peas — larvae develop inside single seed

desc: Large dark rhinoceros beetle — ♂ has three horns on pronotum. Larvae in decaying palm stumps. Attracted to lights. One of the most impressive beetles in SoCal.

fm.Habitat: Decaying palm stumps — attracted to lights

desc: Small mottled bark-patterned beetle with broad snout. Found on dead wood with fungal growth. Larvae feed in fungi and decaying wood. Cryptic — resembles a bit of bark.

fm.Color: Mottled brown/gray bark pattern; broad flat snout

desc: Dark brown darkling beetle. Larvae ('mealworms') sold as pet food worldwide. Adults in stored grain.
